Details: Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation is effective for V. cholera inactivation. One study evaluating low-pressure UV lamps showed one to six log inactivation of V. cholerae was achieved with UV doses ranging from 0.65 to 4.0 mWsec/cm2 [951]. Another study tested V. cholerae inactivation by a point-of-use water treatment system, which consisted of an activated carbon filter followed by a UV reactor [955]. The test system achieved >99.9999 percent (six log) V. cholerae inactivation [955]. A field trial study in Kenya suggested drinking water exposed to solar disinfection helped to protect young children from cholera [1670].
